original_hypothesis.title: Token Issuer Can Arbitrarily Confiscate Any User's Vesting Allocations via transferVesting
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The tokenIssuer authorized on each StepVesting contract can call transferVesting with any _grantor address, unilaterally moving vesting allocations from any beneficiary to any other address without the grantor's consent. Similarly, the VestingDeployer's transferVesting function allows the token owner to specify arbitrary grantor addresses. While the project documentation states 'Token issuers must always be able to reallocate vestings' (confirming this is by design), marketplace buyers who purchase vesting allocations may not realize their purchased allocations can be confiscated by the token issuer at any time. There is no timelock, governance approval, or notification mechanism for such transfers.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Even though documented as intended, marketplace buyers have a reasonable expectation that purchased allocations are secure. The absence of any safeguard (timelock, multi-sig, event alerting) makes this a trust boundary concern for marketplace participants.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(msg.sender == tokenIssuer || msg.sender == manager || msg.sender == vestingDeployer) — tokenIssuer can call with any _grantor, no consent required

preconditions: Token issuer (or token owner via VestingDeployer) decides to confiscate user funds
Target user has non-zero vesting allocation

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: transferVesting accepts arbitrary _grantor from authorized callers; no approval or notification mechanism
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Token issuer role required (semi-trusted per docs)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time token issuer chooses to act

operational_questions: Are marketplace buyers informed that token issuers can confiscate purchased allocations?
Should purchased allocations (tracked via allocations[buyer].bought) be protected from issuer confiscation?
Is a timelock or governance approval appropriate for this power?
validation_requirements: Token issuer calls transferVesting(buyer, attacker, buyer_balance), verify success
Verify buyer loses all purchased tokens with no recourse
Verify no event or notification alerts the buyer

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Malicious or compromised token issuer identifies high-value buyer allocations
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Calls StepVesting.transferVesting(buyer, issuer_address, buyer_balance) or via VestingDeployer
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Buyer's entire vesting allocation transferred to issuer without consent
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Issuer claims or sells the confiscated tokens

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete loss of purchased vesting for affected buyers; marketplace trust destroyed
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Irreversible unless issuer cooperates; no on-chain governance recourse
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All vesting allocations under issuer's vesting plans

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: VestingTransferred events with unexpected grantor (not the beneficiary)
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Buyer vesting balances decreasing without claim or marketplace activity
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Token issuer calling transferVesting with marketplace buyers as grantors

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
